subject: Calculus Prerequisite
question: Determine for which values of $p$ the improper integral $\int^\infty x^p\,dx$ diverges.
explanation: Suppose first that $p = 1$. Then $\int^\infty \frac{1}{x}\,dx = \lim_{b\to\infty} \int_1^b \frac{1}{x}\,dx = \lim_{b\to\infty} \left[\ln|x|\right]_1^b = \lim_{b\to\infty} \ln b = \infty$ so the improper integral is divergent. Now, suppose that $p \ne 1$. Then $\int^\infty x^p\,dx = \lim_{b\to\infty} \int^b x^{-p}\,dx = \lim_{b\to\infty} \left[\frac{x^{-p+1}}{-p + 1}\right]^b = \lim_{b\to\infty} \left(\frac{b^{-p+1}}{-p + 1} - \frac{1}{-p + 1}\right)$. If $p < 1$ then $-p + 1 > 0$ so that $\lim_{b\to\infty} b^{-p+1} = \infty$ and therefore the improper integral is divergent. If $p > 1$ then $-p+1 < 0$ so that $\lim_{b\to\infty} b^{-p+1} = 0$ and hence the improper integral converges: $\int^\infty x^p\,dx = \frac{-1}{-p + 1}$
